mappings.rationaleAI: Al-Ghazali's critique of philosophy, defence of Sunni theology, and embrace of Sufism directly address the Middle Ages of Islam (A.5), Islamic theology and philosophy (B.4), and the mystical path of Sufism (B.5).
The Arab conquests spreading Islam from Arabia across the Middle East, North Africa, and into Iberia directly address the foundations and early expansion of Islam (A.3).
The Abbasid Caliphate's golden age, House of Wisdom, and achievements in arts and sciences directly address the development of Islamic religion, culture, and society during the Abbasid era (A.4).
Islamic law (Sharia) covering the Quran, Hadith, ijma, qiyas, and the four Sunni legal schools directly addresses Islamic law (B.3) and Shari'ah: law and jurisprudence (C.3.d).
The Translation Movement of Greek texts into Arabic during the Abbasid Caliphate directly addresses the development of Islamic intellectual culture (A.4) and philosophical trends in Islam (B.4).
The Safavid Dynasty establishing Shia Islam as state religion with its Sufi origins directly addresses the Middle Ages of Islam (A.5) and Shi'ism (C.2.d).

The Arabian Nights as a collection of Middle Eastern folk tales spanning Arabic, Persian, and Indian sources is relevant to mythical elements of Islamic beliefs (B.6) and Islamic art and cultural expression (C.4).
Antarah ibn Shaddad as a pre-Islamic Arabian poet illuminates the Jahiliyyah literary tradition and pre-Islamic Arabian society — directly relevant to the pre-Islamic setting in Arabia (A.1).
Zoroastrianism's influence on Islam including concepts of heaven, hell, angels, and resurrection is relevant to the pre-Islamic religious background (A.1).
Angels in Islamic tradition including Jibril's role in Quranic revelation directly addresses doctrines concerning intermediate beings in Islam (C.1.d).
Muhammad as the Seal of the Prophets directly addresses doctrines concerning Muhammad and the nature of prophecy (C.1.e).
Islamic Jannah (paradise) as a conception of the afterlife directly addresses Islamic eschatological doctrines (C.1.f).
Islamic fundamentalism and political Islam as reactions to modernity directly address Islam in the modern world (A.6).
The Volga Vikings' trade with the Abbasid Caliphate and Ibn Fadlan's account illustrate the early expansion and cultural interactions of Islam (A.3).

The 12th-century Renaissance covers the recovery of classical texts through Arabic translations and contact with Islamic civilisation — relevant to Islamic intellectual development (A.4) and philosophy (B.4).
Alchemy's transmission through Islamic civilization with figures like Jabir ibn Hayyan is relevant to Islamic philosophical and scientific traditions (B.4).
